699	\subsection*{Triple-overlap rings and the localised transition maps}
700	
701	The cocycle condition \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) cannot
702	be stated as a naive composition of the landed transition maps of
703	\cref{def:gr_transition}: the codomain of \(\theta_{J,K}\) (the ring
704	\(R^J[1/P^J_K]\)) differs from the domain of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (the ring
705	\(R^J[1/P^J_I]\)). The identity therefore lives over the \emph{triple-overlap}
706	rings obtained by inverting both relevant minors in each chart ring:
707	\[
708	  S_K := R^K[1/(P^K_I P^K_J)], \quad
709	  S_J := R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)], \quad
710	  S_I := R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)].
711	\]
712	
713	\begin{definition}
714	
715	\leanok
716	[Inclusion of an away-localisation into a double one (left)]
717	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_left}
718	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclLeft}
719	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
720	  For \(x, y \in R\), the canonical inclusion
721	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y} : R[1/x] \to R[1/(xy)]\) inverting the extra factor
722	  \(y\), defined as the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
723	  the structure map \(R \to R[1/(xy)]\) along \(x\) — well-defined because \(x\)
724	  is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\) (a factor of the unit \(xy\),
725	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}).
726	\end{definition}
727	
728	\begin{definition}
729	
730	\leanok
731	[Inclusion of an away-localisation into a double one (right)]
732	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_right}
733	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclRight}
734	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
735	  For \(x, y \in R\), the canonical inclusion
736	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y} : R[1/y] \to R[1/(xy)]\) inverting the extra factor
737	  \(x\), defined as the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
738	  \(R \to R[1/(xy)]\) along \(y\) — well-defined since \(y\) is a unit of
739	  \(R[1/(xy)]\) (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}).
740	\end{definition}
741	
742	\begin{lemma}
743	
744	\leanok
745	[Left inclusion lies over \(R\)]
746	  \label{lem:gr_awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap}
747	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap}
748	  \uses{def:gr_away_incl_left}
749	  The inclusion \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left})
750	  intertwines the two structure maps:
751	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y} \circ (R \to R[1/x]) = (R \to R[1/(xy)])\).
752	\end{lemma}
753	\begin{proof}
754	
755	\leanok
756	  By the defining property of the away-localisation lift
757	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}). Proved directly in Lean.
758	\end{proof}
759	
760	\begin{lemma}
761	
762	\leanok
763	[Right inclusion lies over \(R\)]
764	  \label{lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap}
765	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap}
766	  \uses{def:gr_away_incl_right}
767	  The inclusion \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_right})
768	  intertwines the two structure maps:
769	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y} \circ (R \to R[1/y]) = (R \to R[1/(xy)])\).
770	\end{lemma}
771	\begin{proof}
772	
773	\leanok
774	  By the defining property of the away-localisation lift
775	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}). Proved directly in Lean.
776	\end{proof}
777	
778	\begin{lemma}
779	
780	\leanok
781	[Left factor is a unit in the double localisation]
782	  \label{lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_left}
783	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_algebraMap_away_left}
784	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}

786	\end{lemma}
787	\begin{proof}
788	
789	\leanok
790	  \(xy\) is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\)
791	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}); a factor of a unit is a unit.
792	  Proved directly in Lean.
793	\end{proof}

795	\begin{lemma}
796	
797	\leanok
798	[Right factor is a unit in the double localisation]
799	  \label{lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_right}
800	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_algebraMap_away_right}
801	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
802	  For \(x, y \in R\), the image of \(y\) in \(R[1/(xy)]\) is a unit.
803	\end{lemma}
804	\begin{proof}
805	
806	\leanok
807	  \(xy\) is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\)
808	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}); a factor of a unit is a unit.
809	  Proved directly in Lean.
810	\end{proof}
811	
812	\begin{lemma}
813	
814	\leanok
815	[The cross minor becomes a unit after inclusion]
816	  \label{lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross}
817	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross}
818	  \uses{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, def:gr_image_matrix}
819	  Let \(A, B, C\) be size-\(d\) subsets and \(\iota : R^A_B \to D\) a ring
820	  homomorphism over the structure map \(R^A \to D\) under which the minor
821	  determinant \(P^A_C\) becomes a unit of \(D\). Then
822	  \(\iota\bigl(\tilde\theta_{A,B}(P^B_C)\bigr)\) is a unit of \(D\). Indeed
823	  \[
824	    \tilde\theta_{A,B}\bigl(P^B_C\bigr)
825	      = \det\bigl((X^A_B)^{-1} X^A_C\bigr)
826	      = \det\bigl((X^A_B)^{-1}\bigr)\cdot P^A_C ,
827	  \]
828	  a product of two units once \(P^A_C\) is inverted in \(D\).
829	\end{lemma}
830	\begin{proof}
831	
832	\leanok
833	  By \cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet},
834	  \(\tilde\theta_{A,B}(P^B_C) = \det(M_C)\) with \(M = (X^A_B)^{-1} X^A\); split
835	  the \(C\)-minor of the product (\cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}) and the
836	  determinant as \(\det((X^A_B)^{-1}) \cdot P^A_C\). The first factor is a unit
837	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}); the second becomes a unit under
838	  \(\iota\) by hypothesis. Proved directly in Lean.
839	\end{proof}
840	
841	\begin{definition}
842	
843	\leanok
844	[Localised transition map \(\Theta_{I,J} : S_J \to S_I\)]
845	  \label{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}
846	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocycleΘIJ}

848	  The triple-overlap transition map
849	  \(\Theta_{I,J} : S_J = R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)] \to S_I = R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)]\):
850	  the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) along the doubly
851	  inverted minor \(P^J_I P^J_K\) of the pre-hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)

853	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}\) into \(S_I\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left}). It is

857	  by \cref{lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross} (with cross factor
858	  \(P^I_K\) a unit, \cref{lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_right}).
859	\end{definition}

895	\begin{lemma}
896	
897	\leanok
898	[Image-matrix identity over the triple overlap]

1001	\begin{proof}
1002	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_transition_self, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_cocycle_theta_jk, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ik, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
1003	  \leanok
1004	  We verify the scheme-morphism form
1005	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) by a direct matrix
1006	  computation, valid over the open locus of \(U^I\) on which all the minors
1007	  involved are invertible (namely \(P^I_J\) and \(P^I_K\), which suffices since
1008	  the maps are determined by their restriction to a dense open and all rings are
1009	  domains; the computation is an identity of matrices with entries in
1010	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J, 1/P^I_K]\)).
1011	
1012	  Start from the matrix \(X^I\) (the universal point of \(U^I\)). By
1013	  \cref{def:gr_transition},
1014	  \[
1015	    M^J \;:=\; \theta_{I,J}(X^I) \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
1016	  \]
1017	  a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(J\)-minor is the identity, hence a point of
1018	  \(U^J\). Its \(K\)-minor is
1019	  \[
1020	    M^J_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_K,
1021	    \qquad\text{so}\qquad
1022	    (M^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J,
1023	  \]
1024	  using \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\) for the invertible \(d \times d\) matrices
1025	  \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\). Applying \(\theta_{J,K}\) to \(M^J\) gives
1026	  \[
1027	    \theta_{J,K}(M^J) \;=\; (M^J_K)^{-1} M^J
1028	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J \,\cdot\, (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I
1029	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I,
1030	  \]
1031	  the middle product \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\) cancelling by
1032	  associativity of matrix multiplication. The right-hand side is precisely
1033	  \(\theta_{I,K}(X^I) = (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\). Thus
1034	  \(\theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,K}\) agree on the
1035	  universal matrix \(X^I\), hence agree as morphisms \(U^I \supseteq U^I_J \cap
1036	  U^I_K \to U^K\). The special case \(K = I\) (resp.\ \(J = I\)) together with
1037	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\) shows each \(\theta_{I,J}\) is an isomorphism onto its
1038	  image with inverse \(\theta_{J,I}\), since
1039	  \(\theta_{J,I} \circ \theta_{I,J} = \theta_{I,I} = \id\).
1040	\end{proof}

954	\subsection{The section-level base-change identity}
955	
956	The section-level identification
957	Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange} below is the single
958	highest-leverage obligation of the affine close, and we prove it \emph{directly on
959	global sections}, with no abstract adjoint-mate tower. In the affine--affine model the
960	base-change map \(\theta\) is, after its domain and codomain are read through the two
961	proved tilde dictionaries, exactly the \(R'\)-base change of the algebraic unit of
962	extension of scalars; post-composing with the canonical regrouping isomorphism
963	\((A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \cong R' \otimes_R M\) identifies it with the canonical
964	base-change comparison, an isomorphism carrying no flatness hypothesis. The computation
965	factors through three named reads — the domain read, the codomain read, and the single
966	\emph{section-level identity} that the conjugated map is the base change of the unit —
967	together with the one Mathlib-absent crux identifying the pullback cone legs with the
968	\(\operatorname{Spec}\)-maps of the tensor inclusions
969	(Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor}). Two Mathlib results are recorded
970	first as dependency anchors.

978	  induced morphisms \(\operatorname{Spec}(S) \to \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) and

980	  the tensor product,

1142	  sections of the codomain \(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M\) are canonically
1143	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) as an \(R'\)-module. Explicitly, the assembled
1144	  isomorphism

1146	    \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} :
1147	    \Gamma\bigl(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M\bigr)
1148	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M

1159	  which are \emph{not} a priori \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-maps; this is where
1160	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor} enters. By
1161	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor}, the first projection \(g'\) is the
1162	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-map of the inclusion \(A \to R' \otimes_R A\), and the second
1163	  projection \(f'\) is the \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-map of \(R' \to R' \otimes_R A\).
1164	  With the legs so identified the affine dictionaries apply:
1165	  the pullback dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} along
1166	  \(A \to R' \otimes_R A\) reads \((g')^*\widetilde M\) as the extension of scalars

1169	  \(f'_*\) of it as restriction of scalars back to \(R'\). The result is
1170	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) as an \(R'\)-module, and composing the two dictionary

1172	  \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\), with \(\mathcal{F}'\) the tilde of
1173	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\).

1238	% --- Supporting lemmas for the codomain read ---
1239	% These helpers establish that pullback along a scheme isomorphism is an equivalence
1240	% of module categories; the adjunction unit of an equivalence is an isomorphism,
1241	% used in the codomain-read computation.
1242	
1243	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1244	[Pullback along an isomorphism is an equivalence of module categories]
1245	  \label{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1246	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1247	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes. Then the pullback functor on
1248	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is
1249	  one half of an equivalence of categories
1250	  \(Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \simeq X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\), with quasi-inverse
1251	  \((f^{-1})^*\).
1252	\end{lemma}
1253	\begin{proof}
1254	
1255	  Assemble the equivalence from the functor \(f^*\) and the candidate inverse
1256	  \((f^{-1})^*\). The unit and counit isomorphisms are built from the pseudofunctor
1257	  coherences of pullback: \((f^{-1})^* \circ f^* \cong (f \circ f^{-1})^* = \mathrm{id}^*
1258	  \cong \mathrm{id}\) and symmetrically, using
1259	  \(f \circ f^{-1} = \mathrm{id}_Y\), \(f^{-1} \circ f = \mathrm{id}_X\)
1260	  (composition-of-pullbacks, congruence under equal maps, and pullback-of-identity).
1261	  The triangle identities are forced by these coherences.
1262	\end{proof}
1263	
1264	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1265	[The pullback functor along an isomorphism is an equivalence]
1266	  \label{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1267	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1268	  \uses{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1269	  Consequently, for an isomorphism \(f : X \to Y\) the functor
1270	  \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is an equivalence of
1271	  categories. In particular its adjunction unit is a natural isomorphism, so
1272	  \((\eta_{f})_{W}\) is invertible on every object \(W\).
1273	\end{lemma}
1274	\begin{proof}
1275	
1276	  The functor underlying the equivalence of
1277	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso} is exactly \(f^*\), so \(f^*\) is an
1278	  equivalence; an equivalence's unit is a natural isomorphism.
1279	\end{proof}

1281	% --- Section-level identity, three pieces: regroup equiv (pure tensor algebra, native
1282	%     R'-linear via Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange) ← section identity (Γ(θ) is the
1283	%     R'-base change of the algebraic unit) ← IsIso corollary (the pinned leaf). ---
1284	
1285	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1286	[Regrouping isomorphism for the section-level mate]
1287	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1288	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1289	  % NOTE: tensor-order convention. The Lean uses Mathlib's `pullbackSpecIso`
1290	  % orientation `A ⊗[R] R'` (so the codomain is `(A ⊗_R R') ⊗_A M` and the generator
1291	  % goes `r' ⊗ m ↦ (1 ⊗ r') ⊗ m`); the prose below writes `R' ⊗_R A`. The two are the
1292	  % same module up to `TensorProduct.comm` — a faithful re-orientation, no content change.
1293	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1294	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1295	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
1296	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1297	  % L933--938).
1298	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1299	  % equality
1300	  % $$
1301	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
1302	  % $$
1303	  % as $R'$-modules."
1304	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1305	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1306	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\) an
1307	  \(A\)-module. There is a bundled \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
1308	  \[
1309	    \operatorname{regroup} :
1310	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
1311	  \]
1312	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the source, sending the generator
1313	  \((r' \otimes 1) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\); equivalently its inverse sends
1314	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
1315	  hypothesis.
1316	\end{lemma}
1317	
1318	\begin{proof}
1319	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1320	  The underlying \(R'\)-linear equivalence is supplied, already as a bundled
1321	  \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over \(R'\), by the natively \(R'\)-linear
1322	  pushout-cancellation equivalence of
1323	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}. Crucially, that helper bundles
1324	  \(R'\)-linearity \emph{internally}: there is no hand-written scalar-compatibility
1325	  obligation, no induction over elementary tensors, and in particular \emph{no} residual
1326	  \(r' \cdot 0 = 0\) bookkeeping branch. The only work left to this proof is therefore to
1327	  promote that \(R'\)-linear equivalence to a morphism of \(R'\)-module \emph{objects} in
1328	  the exact extension/restriction-of-scalars packaging that the codomain and domain reads
1329	  produce. We work in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation
1330	  (the same module as \(R' \otimes_R A\) up to the canonical commutativity isomorphism,
1331	  per the tensor-order convention note).
1332	
1333	  \emph{The lone residual obstruction is the \(A\)-action diamond, resolved at the object
1334	  level.} The pushout-cancellation helper takes its relative tensor product over the
1335	  \emph{canonical} \(A\)-algebra structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\)
1336	  (multiplication in the left factor), whereas the carrier object produced by the
1337	  dictionaries,
1338	  the extension of scalars of \(M\) along the left inclusion \(\iota : A \to A \otimes_R R'\),
1339	  tensors over the \(A\)-module structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\) induced by \(\iota\). These
1340	  two \(A\)-module structures on
1341	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) agree on elements but are distinct as data, so the two relative
1342	  tensor products are distinct types even though their underlying abelian groups and
1343	  their \(R'\)-actions coincide. This diamond is the \emph{only} thing the helper does
1344	  not already discharge, and — because the \(R'\)-linearity is now entirely internal to
1345	  the Mathlib equivalence — it is resolved purely at the carrier level by an
1346	  identity-on-elements \(R'\)-linear bridge
1347	  \[
1348	    eT : (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\operatorname{restr}} M
1349	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1350	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\mathrm{can}} M ,
1351	  \]
1352	  the identity map on generators, which is \(R'\)-linear because both sides carry the
1353	  same \(R'\)-action (restriction of scalars along \(R' \to A \otimes_R R'\)).
1354	
1355	  Composing the bridge \(eT\) with the natively \(R'\)-linear helper equivalence and
1356	  promoting to an isomorphism of module objects yields the desired bundled
1357	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism of \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) objects. Being a composite
1358	  of linear equivalences it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and none of its constituents
1359	  carries a flatness hypothesis.
1360	\end{proof}

1381	  (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}

1507	  affine flat-base-change comparison invertible without inspecting its concrete sheaf assembly.

1510	\begin{lemma}[The iterated mate of a left-adjoint square is the conjugate (Beck--Chevalley)]
1511	  \label{lem:iterated_mateEquiv_conjugateEquiv_mathlib}
1512	  \lean{CategoryTheory.iterated_mateEquiv_conjugateEquiv}
1513	  \mathlibok

1516	  \(\mathrm{adj}_1, \dots, \mathrm{adj}_4\), the iterated mate (the Beck--Chevalley \(2\)-cell of the
1517	  pasted square) of a \(2\)-cell \(\alpha\) equals the conjugate of \(\alpha\) for the two composite
1518	  adjunctions:
1519	  \[
1520	    \operatorname{mate}\bigl(\operatorname{mate}(\alpha)\bigr) \;=\;
1521	      \operatorname{conj}\bigl(\mathrm{adj}_1.\mathrm{adj}_4,\ \mathrm{adj}_3.\mathrm{adj}_2\bigr)\,\alpha .
1522	  \]

1524	  transformation is a natural isomorphism exactly when \(\alpha\) is — the conceptual home of the
1525	  iso-ness of the affine comparison \(g^* f_*\widetilde M \to f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M\), which the
1526	  project realises directly as a conjugate of the pushforward \(\Gamma\)-comparison.

1628	\begin{lemma}
1629	
1630	\leanok
1631	[Pseudofunctoriality of the pullback--pushforward unit]
1632	  \label{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1633	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1634	  \uses{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib, lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib,
1635	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1636	  Let \(a : X_1 \to X_2\) and \(b : X_2 \to X_3\) be composable morphisms of schemes and let
1637	  \(N\) be an \(\mathcal{O}_{X_3}\)-module. Write \(\eta^{h}\) for the unit of the
1638	  pullback--pushforward adjunction \((h^{*}\dashv h_{*})\) attached to a morphism \(h\), and
1639	  \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b}\), \(\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b}\) for the pseudofunctor
1640	  composition coherences. Then
1641	  \[
1642	    \eta^{b}_{N} \;\circ\; b_{*}\bigl(\eta^{a}_{\,b^{*}N}\bigr)
1643	      \;\circ\; (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}_{N}
1644	    \;=\;
1645	    \eta^{a\circ b}_{N} \;\circ\;
1646	      (a\circ b)_{*}\bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}_{N}\bigr),
1647	  \]
1648	  where composites are read left to right. In words: the unit of the composite morphism
1649	  \(a \circ b\), reindexed by the inverse pullback coherence, factors as the \(b\)-unit, followed
1650	  by \(b_{*}\) of the \(a\)-unit, followed by the pushforward coherence. This is the leg-reindex
1651	  engine consumed by Seam~2 (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex}): with \(a\) an
1652	  isomorphism and \(b\) an affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-map it turns the generic projection-leg
1653	  unit into the affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-unit modulo the transparent coherences.
1654	\end{lemma}

1657	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1658	  Apply the unit-across-conjugate coherence Lemma~\ref{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib} to the
1659	  composite adjunction \((b^{*}\dashv b_{*}) . (a^{*}\dashv a_{*})\), the adjunction
1660	  \(\bigl((a\circ b)^{*}\dashv (a\circ b)_{*}\bigr)\), and the left-adjoint transformation
1661	  \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\). This yields an identity whose right-hand factor
1662	  is the conjugate (mate) of \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\); by
1663	  Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib} that mate is exactly the pushforward
1664	  coherence \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}\). Expanding the unit of the composite
1665	  adjunction by Lemma~\ref{lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib} —
1666	  \(\bigl((b^{*}\dashv b_{*}).(a^{*}\dashv a_{*})\bigr)\text{-unit}_N
1667	    = \eta^{b}_N \circ b_{*}(\eta^{a}_{b^{*}N})\) — and reassociating the resulting composite gives
1668	  the stated equation.

1867	  The point is that both sides are read through the same functor instance \(F\): only one
1868	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module category structure is in play, so functoriality applies with no instance
1869	  diamond. The distribution of \(F\) over the four expanded-unit factors and the collapse of the
1870	  transparent middle \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}\) factor are thereby fused into one diamond-free
1871	  identity, the clean building block spliced into the telescoping of
1872	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs}.
1873	\end{lemma}
1874	\begin{proof}
1875	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs_unitExpand,
1876	        lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs_gammaDistribute,
1877	        lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pushforwardComp,
1878	        lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id}
1879	  Expand the bare \((b \circ a)\)-unit by
1880	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs_unitExpand} into its four factors, then distribute
1881	  \(F\) over the composite by
1882	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs_gammaDistribute}
1883	  (\(F(u \circ v) = F u \circ F v\)). Because both sides are expressed through the one functor \(F\),
1884	  this is a direct application of functoriality with no re-elaboration of the underlying category
1885	  structure. Of the four resulting \(F\)-image factors, the third is
1886	  \(F\bigl((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}\bigr)\): its section value is the identity
1887	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id}, the content of
1888	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pushforwardComp}), and \(F\) carries the identity to
1889	  the identity. Collapsing this factor by the unit law and reassociating yields the stated three-factor
1890	  form.
1891	\end{proof}

1901	\begin{lemma}[Conjugation distributes over composition (reassoc simp lemma)]
1902	  \label{lem:conjugateEquiv_comp_mathlib}
1903	  \lean{CategoryTheory.conjugateEquiv_comp}
1904	  \mathlibok
1905	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1906	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1 : L_1 \dashv R_1\), \(\mathrm{adj}_2 : L_2 \dashv R_2\),
1907	  \(\mathrm{adj}_3 : L_3 \dashv R_3\) be adjunctions between the same pair of categories, and let
1908	  \(\alpha : L_2 \Rightarrow L_1\), \(\beta : L_3 \Rightarrow L_2\) be transformations of left adjoints.
1909	  Then the conjugates compose, with order reversed onto the right-adjoint side:
1910	  \[
1911	    \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_2)\,\alpha \;\circ\;
1912	    \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_2,\mathrm{adj}_3)\,\beta
1913	      \;=\; \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_3)\,(\beta \circ \alpha) .
1914	  \]
1915	  Tagged \(\mathtt{@[reassoc (attr := simp)]}\), it is the lead member of the conjugate
1916	  reassociation simp set: it fuses the per-adjunction conjugates of a composite adjunction into the
1917	  conjugate of the composite (the conjugate-side form of the paste-of-mates).
1918	\end{lemma}

1931	  \]

953	  \label{lem:mvPolynomial_isNoetherianRing_fin_mathlib}

959	  multivariate polynomial ring \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0, \dots, r-1\}, R)\)

961	  \(R = \kappa\) a field this makes \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) Noetherian, the

978	\subsubsection*{The internal grading of a homogeneous submodule (split $G_1$)}
979	
980	\begin{lemma}\leanok
981	[$G_1$ (independence) -- the family $n \mapsto M_n \cap p$ is independent]
982	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}
983	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_inf_iSupIndep}
984	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
985	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and let
986	  \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
987	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the family
988	  \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) of \(\kappa\)-subspaces of \(M\) is independent: for each
989	  \(n\),
990	  \[
991	    (M_n \cap p) \;\cap\; \Bigl(\bigsqcup_{m \ne n} (M_m \cap p)\Bigr) \;=\; 0 .
992	  \]
993	  The whole statement lives in the ambient module \(M\): the subspaces
994	  \(M_n \cap p\) are submodules of \(M\), never of a derived carrier \(\smash{\hat p}\).
995	\end{lemma}
996	
997	\begin{proof}
998	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
999	  \leanok
1000	  The components \(M_n\) of the internal decomposition of \(M\) are independent. The
1001	  subfamily \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) consists of submodules with \(M_n \cap p \le M_n\),
1002	  so independence is inherited: a relation among the \(M_n \cap p\) is in particular a
1003	  relation among the \(M_n\), forcing each term to vanish.
1004	\end{proof}
1005	
1006	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1007	[$G_1$ (supremum) -- $\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) = p$ for $p$ homogeneous]
1008	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
1009	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_inf_eq}
1010	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1011	  Let \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
1012	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then
1013	  \[
1014	    \bigsqcup_{n} (M_n \cap p) \;=\; p .
1015	  \]
1016	  Together with the independence of
1017	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}, this is the ambient internal
1018	  direct sum \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\), stated entirely inside \(M\) without
1019	  requiring a direct-sum decomposition of the subtype \(\smash{\hat p}\).
1020	\end{lemma}
1021	
1022	\begin{proof}
1023	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1024	  \leanok
1025	  The inclusion \(\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) \le p\) is clear, since each
1026	  \(M_n \cap p \le p\). For the reverse inclusion, take \(m \in p\) and write
1027	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n\) using the
1028	  internal decomposition of \(M\). Homogeneity of \(p\) gives
1029	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\), hence \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n \cap p\); thus
1030	  \(m\) is a finite sum of elements of the \(M_n \cap p\), i.e.
1031	  \(m \in \bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p)\).
1032	\end{proof}

1034	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient class and its Hilbert function}
1035	
1036	\begin{definition}\leanok
1037	[Subquotient Hilbert data]
1038	  \label{def:graded_subquotientHilb}
1039	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotientHilb}
1040	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum}
1041	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.hilb}
1042	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1043	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
1044	  Fix a graded \(\kappa\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\), i.e.\ \(M\) together with an
1045	  internal decomposition \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) whose components
1046	  \(\mathcal{M}_n\) are finite-dimensional over \(\kappa\). A \emph{subquotient
1047	  datum of length \(r\)} consists of:
1048	  \begin{itemize}
1049	    \item a pair of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N \subseteq M\); and
1050	    \item a family \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to (M \to_\kappa M)\) of pairwise
1051	      commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms, each raising degree by one
1052	      (\(t_i(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\)), each preserving \(N\)
1053	      and \(N'\) (\(t_i N \subseteq N\), \(t_i N' \subseteq N'\)); together with
1054	    \item a finiteness condition: the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a finite module over the
1055	      polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) acting through the \(t_i\) (via the
1056	      ambient \(t\)-stable carriers \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N\),
1057	      \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N'\) of \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}).
1058	  \end{itemize}
1059	  The pair \((N, N')\) represents the subquotient \(N/N'\). Its \emph{ambient
1060	  Hilbert function} is
1061	  \[
1062	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \Bigl(\dim_\kappa (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
1063	      \;-\; \dim_\kappa (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\Bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Q},
1064	  \]
1065	  the difference of the (finite) \(\kappa\)-dimensions of the ambient intersections,
1066	  computed in \(\mathbb{Z}\) and cast to \(\mathbb{Q}\). Since \(N' \le N\) one has
1067	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so
1068	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = \dim_\kappa\bigl((N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\bigr)\)
1069	  is the dimension of the degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\). The split \(G_1\)
1070	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
1071	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) guarantees that these ambient
1072	  intersections assemble the homogeneous submodules \(N, N'\), so the data depends
1073	  only on the ambient families \(n \mapsto N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\),
1074	  \(n \mapsto N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\).
1075	\end{definition}
1076	
1077	\subsubsection*{Ambient homogeneity calculus}
1078	
1079	The kernel/cokernel closure and the degreewise difference identity rest on a small
1080	calculus of how a degree-raising endomorphism interacts with the internal grading and
1081	with the lattice of homogeneous submodules. Throughout, \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\)
1082	is the fixed internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and \(x : M \to M\) is a
1083	\(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism raising degree by one. None of these statements forms a
1084	derived carrier: every object is a submodule of the fixed \(M\).
1085	
1086	\begin{definition}\leanok
1087	[Degree-raising endomorphism]
1088	  \label{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1089	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree}
1090	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module. A
1091	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism \(x : M \to M\) \emph{raises degree by one} when it
1092	  carries each graded piece into the next: \(x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1093	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) for every \(n\). This is the grading-ring-free form of
1094	  ``multiplication by a degree-one element'': it abstracts each of the \(r\)
1095	  commuting degree-one generators \(x \in R_1\) acting on \(M\) into a single
1096	  endomorphism, with no reference to the grading ring \(R\).
1097	\end{definition}
1098	
1099	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1100	[Membership form of degree-raising]
1101	  \label{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1102	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree.mem}
1103	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1104	  If \(x\) raises degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and \(m \in
1105	  \mathcal{M}_n\), then \(x m \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1106	\end{lemma}
1107	
1108	\begin{proof}
1109	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1110	  \leanok
1111	  Immediate from the definition: \(x m \in x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1112	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1113	\end{proof}
1114	
1115	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1116	[Degree-shift commutation]
1117	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1118	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree}
1119	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1120	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then for every \(m \in M\) and every \(i\) the
1121	  degree-\((i+1)\) homogeneous component of \(x m\) equals \(x\) applied to the
1122	  degree-\(i\) component of \(m\):
1123	  \[
1124	    \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) \;=\; x\bigl(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)\bigr) .
1125	  \]
1126	  This load-bearing commutation says a degree-raising endomorphism shifts the
1127	  internal decomposition by exactly one slot; it is what makes preimages and images
1128	  of homogeneous submodules under \(x\) homogeneous.
1129	\end{lemma}
1130	
1131	\begin{proof}
1132	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1133	  \leanok
1134	  Decompose \(m = \sum_j \mathrm{proj}_j(m)\) into its finitely many homogeneous
1135	  components and apply \(x\): \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with
1136	  \(x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m)) \in \mathcal{M}_{j+1}\) by
1137	  \cref{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}. Reading off the degree-\((i+1)\) component, all
1138	  terms with \(j+1 \ne i+1\) drop out, leaving \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\).
1139	\end{proof}
1140	
1141	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1142	[Degree-raising kills the bottom]
1143	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1144	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1145	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1146	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then the degree-\(0\) component of \(x m\) vanishes
1147	  for every \(m\): \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0\).
1148	\end{lemma}
1149	
1150	\begin{proof}
1151	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1152	  \leanok
1153	  Writing \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with each summand in
1154	  \(\mathcal{M}_{j+1}\), no summand has degree \(0\) (every \(j+1 \ge 1\)), so the
1155	  degree-\(0\) component is \(0\).
1156	\end{proof}
1157	
1158	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1159	[Preimage of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1160	  \label{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1161	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_isHomogeneous}
1162	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1163	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N' \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
1164	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the preimage \(x^{-1}(N') =
1165	  \{m : x m \in N'\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of \(M\).
1166	\end{lemma}
1167	
1168	\begin{proof}
1169	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1170	  \leanok
1171	  Let \(m \in x^{-1}(N')\) and fix a degree \(i\). By the degree-shift commutation
1172	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}), \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)) =
1173	  \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m)\), which lies in \(N'\) because \(x m \in N'\) and \(N'\)
1174	  is homogeneous. Hence \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\), so \(x^{-1}(N')\) is
1175	  homogeneous.
1176	\end{proof}
1177	
1178	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1179	[Image of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1180	  \label{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1181	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_isHomogeneous}
1182	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1183	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1184	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule.
1185	  Then the image \(x \cdot N = \{x m : m \in N\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of
1186	  \(M\), with degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(x(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) and zero
1187	  degree-\(0\) piece.
1188	\end{lemma}
1189	
1190	\begin{proof}
1191	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1192	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1193	    \leanok
1194	  Take \(x m \in x \cdot N\) with \(m \in N\) and fix a degree. In degree \(0\) the
1195	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0 \in x \cdot N\)
1196	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}). In degree \(i+1\),
1197	  \(\mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\)
1198	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); since \(N\) is homogeneous,
1199	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in N\), so this component lies in \(x \cdot N\). Thus every
1200	  homogeneous component of \(x m\) lies in \(x \cdot N\), which is therefore
1201	  homogeneous.
1202	\end{proof}
1203	
1204	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1205	[Intersection of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1206	  \label{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}
1207	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.inf_isHomogeneous}
1208	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1209	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules
1210	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) then so is their intersection \(p
1211	  \cap q\). Mathlib provides no lattice-closure lemmas for homogeneous submodules, so
1212	  this is recorded here.
1213	\end{lemma}
1214	
1215	\begin{proof}
1216	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1217	  \leanok
1218	  For \(m \in p \cap q\) and any degree \(i\), homogeneity of \(p\) and of \(q\)
1219	  gives \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in q\), hence
1220	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p \cap q\).
1221	\end{proof}
1222	
1223	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1224	[Sum of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1225	  \label{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1226	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_isHomogeneous}
1227	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1228	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules then so is their sum \(p + q\).
1229	\end{lemma}
1230	
1231	\begin{proof}
1232	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1233	  \leanok
1234	  Write an element of \(p + q\) as \(a + b\) with \(a \in p\), \(b \in q\). For any
1235	  degree \(i\), \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) = \mathrm{proj}_i(a) + \mathrm{proj}_i(b)\)
1236	  with \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(b) \in q\) by homogeneity,
1237	  so \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) \in p + q\).
1238	\end{proof}
1239	
1240	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1241	[Ambient image identity]
1242	  \label{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}
1243	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_inf_degree_eq}
1244	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1245	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be homogeneous. Then the
1246	  degree-\((n+1)\) part of the image \(x \cdot N\) is exactly \(x\) of the
1247	  degree-\(n\) part of \(N\):
1248	  \[
1249	    (x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \;=\; x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) .
1250	  \]
1251	\end{lemma}
1252	
1253	\begin{proof}
1254	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1255	  \leanok
1256	  \(\supseteq\): for \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), \(x m \in x \cdot N\) and \(x m
1257	  \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) since \(x\) raises degree. \(\subseteq\): an element \(x m
1258	  \in x \cdot N\) lying in \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) equals its own degree-\((n+1)\)
1259	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_{n+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_n(m))\)
1260	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); homogeneity of \(N\) puts
1261	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so \(x m \in x \cdot (N \cap
1262	  \mathcal{M}_n)\). The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous by
1263	  \cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}.
1264	\end{proof}
1265	
1266	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1267	[Ambient distributive law]
1268	  \label{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1269	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_inf_degree_eq}
1270	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1271	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1272	  For homogeneous submodules \(P, Q \subseteq M\) and any degree \(k\), intersecting
1273	  the sum with a graded piece distributes:
1274	  \[
1275	    (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k \;=\; (P \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k) .
1276	  \]
1277	\end{lemma}
1278	
1279	\begin{proof}
1280	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1281	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1282	    \leanok
1283	  \(\supseteq\) is the general lattice inequality. For \(\subseteq\), take \(z = p +
1284	  q \in (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k\) with \(p \in P\), \(q \in Q\). Since \(z \in
1285	  \mathcal{M}_k\), \(z = \mathrm{proj}_k(z) = \mathrm{proj}_k(p) +
1286	  \mathrm{proj}_k(q)\); homogeneity of \(P\) and \(Q\)
1287	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) puts \(\mathrm{proj}_k(p) \in P \cap
1288	  \mathcal{M}_k\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_k(q) \in Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k\), so \(z \in (P
1289	  \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k)\). The intersection and sum stay in
1290	  the homogeneous lattice by \cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous} and
1291	  \cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}.
1292	\end{proof}
1293	
1294	\subsubsection*{Closure under kernel and cokernel}
1295	
1296	The kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class is realised by eight ambient
1297	component facts: for a degree-raising endomorphism \(x\) and a homogeneous pair
1298	\(N' \le N\), the kernel pair is \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel pair is
1299	\((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\). The lemmas below record that both new pairs are homogeneous,
1300	nest correctly, are annihilated by \(x\), and are preserved by any endomorphism \(t\)
1301	commuting with \(x\) that preserves the original pair.
1302	
1303	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1304	[Homogeneity of the kernel pair's lower module]
1305	  \label{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}
1306	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_isHomogeneous}
1307	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1308	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and let \(N, N'\) be
1309	  homogeneous submodules of \(M\). Then the kernel pair's lower module
1310	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous. Indeed it is the intersection
1311	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous \(N\) with the homogeneous
1312	  preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}).
1313	\end{lemma}
1314	
1315	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1316	[Homogeneity of the cokernel pair's upper module]
1317	  \label{lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous}
1318	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_isHomogeneous}
1319	  \uses{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1320	  Under the same hypotheses, the cokernel pair's upper module \(N' + x \cdot N\) is
1321	  homogeneous: it is the sum (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous
1322	  \(N'\) with the homogeneous image \(x \cdot N\)
1323	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}).
1324	\end{lemma}
1325	
1326	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1327	[The kernel pair nests]
1328	  \label{lem:graded_ker_le}
1329	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_le}
1330	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N'\) (\(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)), then
1331	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed \(N' \le N\) by hypothesis, and \(x \cdot N'
1332	  \subseteq N'\) is exactly \(N' \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\); so \(N'\) is below the
1333	  intersection. Hence the kernel pair \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) is a valid
1334	  subquotient pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1335	\end{lemma}
1336	
1337	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1338	[The cokernel pair nests]
1339	  \label{lem:graded_coker_le}
1340	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_le}
1341	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N\) (\(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)), then
1342	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\): both summands lie in \(N\). Hence the cokernel pair
1343	  \((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) is a valid subquotient pair
1344	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1345	\end{lemma}
1346	
1347	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1348	[$x$ annihilates the kernel subquotient]
1349	  \label{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}
1350	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_annihilate}
1351	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries the kernel pair's lower module into \(N'\):
1352	  \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\). Indeed \(N \cap x^{-1}(N') \subseteq
1353	  x^{-1}(N')\), and applying \(x\) to a preimage of \(N'\) lands in \(N'\). Thus \(x\)
1354	  acts as zero on the kernel subquotient.
1355	\end{lemma}
1356	
1357	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1358	[$x$ annihilates the cokernel subquotient]
1359	  \label{lem:graded_coker_annihilate}
1360	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_annihilate}
1361	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries \(N\) into the cokernel pair's upper module:
1362	  \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\), trivially (the right summand). Thus \(x\)
1363	  acts as zero on the cokernel subquotient \(N/(N' + x \cdot N)\).
1364	\end{lemma}
1365	
1366	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1367	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the preimage]
1368	  \label{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1369	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_map_le_of_commute}
1370	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N'\) (\(t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)). Then
1371	  \(t\) preserves the preimage: \(t \cdot x^{-1}(N') \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed for
1372	  \(m\) with \(x m \in N'\) one has \(x(t m) = t(x m) \in t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\), so
1373	  \(t m \in x^{-1}(N')\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the kernel
1374	  pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\).
1375	\end{lemma}
1376	
1377	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1378	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the image]
1379	  \label{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}
1380	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_map_le_of_commute}
1381	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N\) (\(t \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then
1382	  \(t\) preserves the image: \(t \cdot (x \cdot N) \subseteq x \cdot N\). Indeed for
1383	  \(m \in N\) one has \(t(x m) = x(t m)\) with \(t m \in N\), so \(t(x m) \in x \cdot
1384	  N\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the cokernel pair's upper
1385	  module \(N' + x \cdot N\).
1386	\end{lemma}
1387	
1388	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1389	[Kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class]
1390	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1391	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.ker}
1392	  % This is a narrative grouping lemma: its content is realised in Lean by the eight
1393	  % ambient component facts cited in \uses{} below (no single Lean declaration),
1394	  % together with the SubquotientDatum.ker/.coker constructors that bundle them into a
1395	  % length-(r-1) datum. The `\lean` pin names the `.ker` constructor — the single decl most
1396	  % representative of the bundled closure — so this dependency-bearing grouping node (which is
1397	  % `\uses`'d by the keystone rationality induction) stays wired into the graph rather than
1398	  % orphaning the component facts it `\uses`. This is a deliberate duplicate of the
1399	  % `def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker` pin.
1400	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1401	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1402	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1403	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1404	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1405	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1406	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1407	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1408	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1409	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1410	  endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and set \(x = t_{r-1}\). Form the two new
1411	  pairs of homogeneous submodules of \(M\)
1412	  \[
1413	    K \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr),
1414	    \qquad
1415	    C \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr),
1416	  \]
1417	  where \(x^{-1}(N') = \{\,m \in M : x \cdot m \in N'\,\}\) is the preimage and
1418	  \(x \cdot N\) is the image of \(N\) under \(x\). Then:
1419	  \begin{enumerate}
1420	    \item \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N\) are homogeneous submodules of
1421	      \(M\), with \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\), so both
1422	      \(K\) and \(C\) are subquotient pairs;
1423	    \item the remaining endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\) pairwise commute,
1424	      raise degree by one, and preserve both members of \(K\) and of \(C\); and
1425	    \item \(x\) annihilates both subquotients: \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\)
1426	      and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\).
1427	  \end{enumerate}
1428	  Consequently \(K\) and \(C\) are again subquotient data, now of length \(r-1\)
1429	  (the endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\)), and \(K = \ker x\) and \(C =
1430	  \operatorname{coker} x\) on the subquotient \(N/N'\): \(K\) represents
1431	  \(\ker\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\) and \(C\) represents
1432	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\). No quotient or subtype
1433	  carrier is constructed; \(K\) and \(C\) are ambient pairs of homogeneous
1434	  submodules of the fixed \(M\).
1435	\end{lemma}
1436	
1437	\begin{proof}
1438	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1439	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1440	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1441	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1442	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1443	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1444	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1445	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1446	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1447	    \leanok
1448	  \emph{Homogeneity.} The preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous: if
1449	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(x \cdot m \in N'\), then since \(x\) raises
1450	  degree by one (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) the components
1451	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) lie in distinct degrees, and
1452	  homogeneity of \(N'\) (split \(G_1\),
1453	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) forces each
1454	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N'\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\).
1455	  Thus \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is an intersection of homogeneous submodules, hence
1456	  homogeneous. The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) piece
1457	  \(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) by
1458	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, and a sum of homogeneous
1459	  submodules is homogeneous (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib} applied at the
1460	  module level), so \(N' + x \cdot N\) is homogeneous. The inclusions
1461	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (as \(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)) and
1462	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (as \(N' \le N\) and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)) are
1463	  immediate. In the language of the ambient homogeneity calculus, this paragraph is
1464	  exactly: \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}),
1465	  \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}), and the
1466	  homogeneous lattice is closed under intersection
1467	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) and sum
1468	  (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}).
1469	
1470	  \emph{Preservation.} Each \(t_i\) with \(i \le r-2\) commutes with \(x\) and
1471	  preserves \(N, N'\); hence it preserves \(x^{-1}(N')\) (if \(x t_i m = t_i x m \in
1472	  N'\) whenever \(x m \in N'\)), so it preserves \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\), and it
1473	  preserves \(x \cdot N\) (as \(t_i x N = x t_i N \subseteq x N\)), so it preserves
1474	  \(N' + x \cdot N\). Degree-raising and commutativity are inherited.
1475	
1476	  \emph{Annihilation by \(x\).} For \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) one has
1477	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\) by definition, so \(x\) carries the first member of \(K\) into
1478	  its second; and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\) trivially. Thus \(x\) acts as
1479	  zero on \(N/N'\) restricted to \(K\) and on the cokernel quotient \(C\).
1480	
1481	  \emph{Identification with \(\ker x\) and \(\operatorname{coker} x\).} On the
1482	  subquotient \(N/N'\), the class of \(m \in N\) is killed by \(x\) iff
1483	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\), i.e.\ iff \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\); so \(\ker(x : N/N' \to
1484	  N/N')\) is exactly \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\), represented by \(K\). The image of
1485	  \(x\) on \(N/N'\) is \((N' + x \cdot N)/N'\), so the cokernel
1486	  \((N/N')/\operatorname{im} x = N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) is represented by \(C\).
1487	\end{proof}
1488	
1489	\subsubsection*{The subquotient degreewise difference identity ($D_6$)}
1490	
1491	\begin{lemma}
1492	
1493	\leanok
1494	[preimage dimension equals ambient-intersection dimension]
1495	  \label{lem:graded_finrank_comap_subtype}
1496	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.finrank_comap_subtype}
1497	  For submodules \(p, S\) of a \(\kappa\)-vector space \(M\), the dimension of the
1498	  preimage of \(S\) under the inclusion \(p \hookrightarrow M\) equals the dimension of
1499	  the ambient intersection: \(\dim_\kappa(\iota_p^{-1} S) = \dim_\kappa(p \cap S)\),
1500	  where \(\iota_p : p \to M\) is the canonical injection. Project-local helper consumed
1501	  by the two kernel-dimension computations of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}.
1502	\end{lemma}
1503	\begin{proof}
1504	
1505	\leanok
1506	  The inclusion \(\iota_p\) is injective, so it restricts to a \(\kappa\)-linear
1507	  isomorphism from \(\iota_p^{-1} S\) onto its image \(\iota_p(\iota_p^{-1} S) = p \cap S\)
1508	  (image of a comap under an injective map). An isomorphism preserves dimension, giving
1509	  the claimed equality.
1510	\end{proof}
1511	
1512	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1513	[$D_6$ -- subquotient degreewise difference]
1514	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1516	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1517	    lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1518	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:graded_finrank_comap_subtype,
1519	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1520	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1521	  % NOTE: The proof factors the two kernel-dimension computations through the
1522	  %   helper \cref{lem:graded_finrank_comap_subtype} (preimage dimension = ambient
1523	  %   intersection dimension).
1524	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
1525	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1526	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1527	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (degreewise short exact sequence).}
1528	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1529	  Hilbert function \(h_M\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Let \(h_K\) and \(h_C\) be the
1530	  Hilbert functions of the kernel and cokernel subquotients
1531	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) of
1532	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}. Then for every \(n\)
1533	  \[
1534	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1535	  \]
1536	  Writing \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) for the
1537	  degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\) (so \(\dim_\kappa Q_n = h_M(n)\)), multiplication by
1538	  \(x\) is a \(\kappa\)-linear map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) with
1539	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\) and
1540	  \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x\) the degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\); the identity is
1541	  then the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1542	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}). Specialising to \((N, N') = (\top,
1543	  \bot)\) recovers the classical \(0 \to \mathcal{M}_n \xrightarrow{x}
1544	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x\mathcal{M}_n \to 0\).
1545	\end{lemma}
1546	
1547	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
1548	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1549	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
1550	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
1551	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
1552	%  following short exact sequence
1553	%  $$
1554	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
1555	%  $$
1556	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
1557	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
1558	\begin{proof}
1559	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
1560	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1561	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1562	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1563	    \leanok
1564	  Since \(N' \le N\) are homogeneous, the split \(G_1\)
1565	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) identifies the degree-\(n\) piece
1566	  of \(N/N'\) with \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\), a
1567	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space of dimension \(h_M(n)\). Because \(x\)
1568	  raises degree by one and preserves \(N\) and \(N'\)
1569	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), it induces a \(\kappa\)-linear
1570	  map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\).
1571	
1572	  \emph{Kernel.} A class \([m] \in Q_n\) (with \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)) lies in
1573	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) iff \(x \cdot m \in N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\), i.e.\
1574	  iff \(m \in (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\). Hence \(\ker(x : Q_n \to
1575	  Q_{n+1}) = \bigl((N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\bigr)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1576	  is exactly the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\), of dimension \(h_K(n)\)
1577	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
1578	
1579	  \emph{Cokernel.} The image of \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) is
1580	  \(\bigl(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) + (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\bigr)/(N' \cap
1581	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\). Using the ambient identity \((N' + x \cdot N) \cap
1582	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}) + x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1583	  -- which is the ambient distributive law (\cref{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq})
1584	  applied to \(P = N'\), \(Q = x \cdot N\), combined with the ambient image identity
1585	  \((x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1586	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}) -- the cokernel \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x = (N \cap
1587	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})/\bigl((N' + x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\bigr)\) is the
1588	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\), of dimension \(h_C(n+1)\).
1589	
1590	  \emph{Difference.} Apply the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1591	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) to \(\varphi = (x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\),
1592	  with \(V = Q_n\), \(W = Q_{n+1}\):
1593	  \[
1594	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n)
1595	      = \dim_\kappa Q_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa Q_n
1596	      = \dim_\kappa(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
1597	      = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1598	  \]
1599	  This is the \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) hypothesis consumed by \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}.
1600	\end{proof}
1601	
1602	\subsubsection*{The free polynomial-ring module structure}
1603	
1604	The finiteness condition of a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) and
1605	its transfer down one generator (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1606	phrased over the \emph{free} polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] =
1607	\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0, \dots, r-1\}, \kappa)\). The following blocks build the
1608	action of that ring on the ambient module \(M\) from the \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1609	degree-raising endomorphisms \(t_i\), keeping every carrier an ambient submodule of
1610	\(M\). The free polynomial ring -- rather than the subalgebra
1611	\(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1612	-- is used precisely so the inductive transfer has the ring surjection
1613	\(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\) available
1614	(\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
1615	
1616	\begin{definition}\leanok
1617	[Polynomial evaluation ring homomorphism]
1618	  \label{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1619	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom}
1620	  \uses{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1621	  Let \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) be a family of
1622	  pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms. The \emph{polynomial evaluation
1623	  ring homomorphism}
1624	  \[
1625	    \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t) : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \longrightarrow
1626	      \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)
1627	  \]
1628	  sends each variable \(X_i\) to \(t_i\). It is constructed by evaluating into the
1629	  \emph{commutative} \(\kappa\)-subalgebra \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa(\{t_i\})
1630	  \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) -- commutative by
1631	  \cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib} because the generators commute -- and then
1632	  including \(A \hookrightarrow \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); evaluation into the
1633	  noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) directly is not an algebra
1634	  homomorphism, which is why the commutative \(A\) is interposed.
1635	\end{definition}
1636	
1637	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1638	[Evaluation sends $X_i$ to $t_i$]
1639	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}
1640	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_X}
1641	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1642	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(X_i) = t_i\) for each \(i\). Immediate from the construction
1643	  (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}), as evaluation sends \(X_i\) to the chosen image
1644	  \(t_i\).
1645	\end{lemma}
1646	
1647	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1648	[Evaluation sends a constant to a scalar]
1649	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1650	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_C}
1651	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1652	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(C\,c) = c \cdot \mathrm{id}_M\) for each \(c \in \kappa\):
1653	  the evaluation homomorphism (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}) is \(\kappa\)-linear and
1654	  carries the constant polynomial \(C\,c\) to the scalar endomorphism \(c \cdot 1\).
1655	\end{lemma}
1656	
1657	\begin{definition}\leanok
1658	[Polynomial-ring module structure on $M$]
1659	  \label{def:graded_polyModule}
1660	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule}
1661	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1662	  The \emph{polynomial-module structure} is the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module
1663	  structure on \(M\) obtained by restricting scalars along
1664	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)\) (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}): a polynomial \(p\) acts on
1665	  \(m \in M\) by \(p \cdot m = \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(p)(m)\). This is the module over the
1666	  free polynomial ring on which the ambient subquotient induction's finiteness condition
1667	  is measured.
1668	\end{definition}
1669	
1670	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1671	[$X_i$ acts as $t_i$]
1672	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul}
1673	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_X_smul}
1674	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}
1675	  In the polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}), \(X_i \cdot m =
1676	  t_i(m)\) for all \(m \in M\). Immediate from
1677	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(X_i) = t_i\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}).
1678	\end{lemma}
1679	
1680	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1681	[A constant acts as a scalar]
1682	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}
1683	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_C_smul}
1684	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1685	  In the polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}), \((C\,c) \cdot m =
1686	  c \cdot m\) for all \(c \in \kappa\), \(m \in M\). Immediate from
1687	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(C\,c) = c \cdot \mathrm{id}_M\)
1688	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}).
1689	\end{lemma}
1690	
1691	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1692	[$\kappa$-scalar tower]
1693	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_isScalarTower}
1694	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_isScalarTower}
1695	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1696	  The polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}) is compatible with the
1697	  original \(\kappa\)-action: \(\mathrm{IsScalarTower}\,\kappa\,\kappa[t_0, \dots,
1698	  t_{r-1}]\,M\). Indeed the algebra map \(\kappa \to \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)
1699	  followed by the polynomial action recovers the \(\kappa\)-action, since a scalar
1700	  \(c \in \kappa\) maps to the constant \(C\,c\), which acts as \(c\)
1701	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}).
1702	\end{lemma}
1703	
1704	\begin{definition}\leanok
1705	[Polynomial-ring submodule from a $t$-stable submodule]
1706	  \label{def:graded_polySubmodule}
1707	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polySubmodule}
1708	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul,
1709	    lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}
1710	  Let \(N \subseteq M\) be a \(\kappa\)-submodule that is stable under each \(t_i\)
1711	  (\(t_i \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then \(N\), with the \emph{same} ambient carrier, is a
1712	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-submodule of \(M\) for the polynomial-module structure
1713	  (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}). Stability under the action of an arbitrary polynomial
1714	  follows by induction on monomials: constants act as scalars
1715	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}) and multiplication by \(X_i\) acts as \(t_i\)
1716	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul}), both of which preserve \(N\).
1717	\end{definition}
1718	
1719	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1720	[The polynomial submodule has the same carrier]
1721	  \label{lem:graded_polySubmodule_coe}
1722	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polySubmodule_coe}
1723	  \uses{def:graded_polySubmodule}
1724	  The underlying set of \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}(t, N)\)
1725	  (\cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}) equals \(N\): the lift to a
1726	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-submodule does not change the carrier, so every object
1727	  in the induction remains an ambient submodule of the fixed \(M\).
1728	\end{lemma}
1729	
1730	\subsubsection*{Finiteness-transfer infrastructure}
1731	
1732	The ambient finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer})
1733	factors the polynomial action of the larger ring through a surjection onto the
1734	smaller one. The following blocks build that surjection and the semilinearity
1735	identity it rests on, and record the two elementary finiteness manipulations
1736	(shrinking a numerator, enlarging a denominator) used to feed the kernel and
1737	cokernel constructors.
1738	
1739	\begin{definition}
1740	
1741	\leanok
1742	[The last-variable surjection of polynomial rings]
1743	  \label{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}
1744	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom}
1745	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_X_castSucc}
1746	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_X_last}
1747	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_C}
1748	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_rename_castSucc}
1749	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_surjective}
1750	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_ringHomSurjective}
1751	  \uses{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1752	  For a field \(\kappa\) and \(r \in \mathbb{N}\), the \emph{last-variable
1753	  homomorphism} is the \(\kappa\)-algebra map
1754	  \[
1755	    \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1756	      = \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0,\dots,r\}, \kappa)
1757	      \;\longrightarrow\;
1758	      \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0,\dots,r-1\}, \kappa) = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]
1759	  \]
1760	  obtained by evaluation: it sends the last variable \(X_r\) to \(0\) and each
1761	  earlier variable \(X_i\) (for \(0 \le i < r\)) to \(X_i\), and fixes constants.
1762	  It is a left inverse of the algebra map
1763	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \to \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)
1764	  induced by the canonical inclusion \(\{0, \dots, r-1\} \hookrightarrow \{0, \dots, r\}\)
1765	  on variable indices, and is therefore surjective. This is the concrete
1766	  ring surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r] \twoheadrightarrow
1767	  \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) along which finiteness is transferred
1768	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
1769	\end{definition}
1770	
1771	\begin{lemma}
1772	
1773	\leanok
1774	[A $t$-stable submodule is closed under the polynomial action]
1775	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_mem_of_stable}
1776	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_mem_of_stable}
1777	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_X, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1778	  Let \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\) be pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms
1779	  of \(M\) and let \(P' \subseteq M\) be a \(\kappa\)-submodule stable under each
1780	  \(t_i\) (\(t_i \cdot P' \subseteq P'\)). Then \(P'\) is closed under the action of
1781	  every polynomial: for all \(p \in \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) and \(m \in P'\),
1782	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(p)(m) \in P'\) (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}). The proof
1783	  is induction on \(p\): a constant acts as a scalar (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}),
1784	  multiplication by a variable acts as the stabilising \(t_i\)
1785	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}), and sums are handled additively.
1786	\end{lemma}
1787	
1788	\begin{lemma}
1789	
1790	\leanok
1791	[Mod-$P'$ semilinearity of the polynomial action]
1792	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}
1793	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}
1794	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_X, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C,
1795	    lem:graded_polyEndHom_mem_of_stable, lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}
1796	  Let \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) be pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms of
1797	  \(M\), and let \(P' \le P\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules each stable under every
1798	  \(t_i\), with the last endomorphism \(x = t_r\) carrying \(P\) into \(P'\)
1799	  (\(x \cdot P \subseteq P'\)). Then for every polynomial
1800	  \(s \in \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\) and every \(m \in P\),
1801	  \[
1802	    \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(s)(m)
1803	      \;-\; \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})
1804	        \bigl(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}(s)\bigr)(m)
1805	      \;\in\; P' .
1806	  \]
1807	  In words: acting by \(s\) through the full family \(t\) agrees, modulo \(P'\), with
1808	  first projecting away the last variable via \(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}\)
1809	  (\cref{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}) and acting through the reduced family
1810	  \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\). This is the semilinearity identity that makes the
1811	  \(\sigma\)-semilinear quotient map of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1812	  well-defined. The proof is induction on \(s\): the case of an earlier variable
1813	  \(s = X_i\) (\(i < r\)) is the inductive hypothesis after \(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}\)
1814	  fixes it; the case of the last variable \(s = X_r\) annihilates the reduced term,
1815	  and the full term lands in \(P'\) because \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into the
1816	  \(t\)-stable \(P'\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_mem_of_stable}).
1817	\end{lemma}
1818	
1819	\begin{lemma}
1820	
1821	\leanok
1822	[Shrinking the numerator preserves finiteness]
1823	  \label{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}
1824	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}
1825	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1826	    lem:module_finite_of_injective_mathlib,
1827	    lem:mvPolynomial_isNoetherianRing_fin_mathlib,
1828	    lem:isNoetherian_of_isNoetherianRing_of_finite_mathlib}
1829	  Let \(N_1 \le N_2\) and \(P'\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules of \(M\), each stable under
1830	  the commuting family \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and suppose the subquotient
1831	  \(N_2/P'\) is finite over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)
1832	  (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}, \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). Then \(N_1/P'\) is
1833	  also finite over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\). Indeed the inclusion
1834	  \(N_1 \subseteq N_2\) induces an injective \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-linear
1835	  map \(N_1/P' \hookrightarrow N_2/P'\); since
1836	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) is Noetherian
1837	  (\cref{lem:mvPolynomial_isNoetherianRing_fin_mathlib}), the finite module
1838	  \(N_2/P'\) is Noetherian
1839	  (\cref{lem:isNoetherian_of_isNoetherianRing_of_finite_mathlib}), so its submodule
1840	  \(N_1/P'\) is finite (\cref{lem:module_finite_of_injective_mathlib}). This supplies
1841	  the finiteness witness of the kernel constructor
1842	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker}).
1843	\end{lemma}
1844	
1845	\begin{lemma}
1846	
1847	\leanok
1848	[Enlarging the denominator preserves finiteness]
1849	  \label{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}
1850	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}
1851	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1852	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}
1853	  Let \(N\) and \(P_1 \le P_2\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules of \(M\), each stable under
1854	  the commuting family \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and suppose the subquotient
1855	  \(N/P_1\) is finite over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)
1856	  (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}, \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). Then \(N/P_2\) is
1857	  also finite over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\). Indeed the inclusion
1858	  \(P_1 \subseteq P_2\) gives a surjection \(N/P_1 \twoheadrightarrow N/P_2\)
1859	  (\cref{lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}), and finiteness transfers along it
1860	  (\cref{lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}). This supplies the
1861	  finiteness witness of the cokernel constructor
1862	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}).
1863	\end{lemma}
1864	
1865	\subsubsection*{Ambient finiteness transfer}
1866	
1867	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1868	[Abstract single-pair finiteness transfer down one variable]
1869	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1870	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_finite_transfer}
1871	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1872	    lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem,
1873	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib,
1874	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1875	  Let \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) be pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms of
1876	  \(M\), and let \(P' \le P\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules of \(M\), each stable under
1877	  every \(t_i\). Suppose the last endomorphism \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into \(P'\)
1878	  (\(x \cdot P \subseteq P'\)), and that the subquotient \(P/P'\) is finite over the
1879	  free polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\) for the polynomial-module structure
1880	  of \cref{def:graded_polyModule} (with carriers the ambient \(t\)-stable submodules
1881	  \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\), \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\),
1882	  \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). Then \(P/P'\) is finite over
1883	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) for the reduced family \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\).
1884	
1885	  This is the abstract single-pair transfer: it does not mention the kernel/cokernel
1886	  pairs, but is the engine applied to each of them by the kernel and cokernel
1887	  constructors (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker},
1888	  \cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}). Because the last variable
1889	  annihilates the subquotient, the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-action factors through
1890	  the last-variable surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r] \twoheadrightarrow
1891	  \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), which is what makes the transfer drop one variable.
1892	\end{lemma}
1893	
1894	\begin{proof}
1895	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1896	    lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem,
1897	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}
1898	    \leanok
1899	  Write \(\sigma = \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1900	  \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) for the surjective ring
1901	  homomorphism sending the last variable to \(0\) and each earlier variable \(X_i\)
1902	  (\(i < r\)) to \(X_i\) (\cref{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}). Consider the map on numerators
1903	  \[
1904	    g : \mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P \;\longrightarrow\;
1905	      \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/
1906	        \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr), \qquad y \longmapsto [\,y\,],
1907	  \]
1908	  where the source carries the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-module structure and the
1909	  target the reduced \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module structure (both with the
1910	  same ambient carrier \(P\), \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). The mod-\(P'\)
1911	  semilinearity identity (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}) -- acting by
1912	  \(s\) through the full family and acting by \(\sigma(s)\) through the reduced family
1913	  agree modulo \(P'\), precisely because \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into \(P'\) --
1914	  says exactly that \(g\) is \(\sigma\)-semilinear. The map \(g\) is surjective (it is
1915	  the quotient projection on the common carrier), and it kills the denominator
1916	  \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\), so it descends through that submodule
1917	  (\cref{lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}) to a surjective
1918	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-linear map
1919	  \[
1920	    \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/\bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr)
1921	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\;
1922	    \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/\bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr)
1923	  \]
1924	  from the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finite subquotient onto the reduced one.
1925	  Finiteness transfers along this surjection
1926	  (\cref{lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}), giving finiteness of \(P/P'\)
1927	  over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\).
1928	
1929	  \emph{Why the free polynomial ring.} The smaller ring must be the \emph{free}
1930	  polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), not the subalgebra
1931	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq
1932	  \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\): relations among the endomorphisms inside
1933	  \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) would obstruct the polynomial-ring surjection
1934	  \(\sigma\) that the scalar transfer requires
1935	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}). Keeping free polynomial
1936	  rings at every step keeps \(\sigma\) -- and hence the transfer -- available
1937	  throughout. Everything is finite generation of ambient submodules of the fixed
1938	  \(M\) and their quotients.
1939	\end{proof}
1940	
1941	\subsubsection*{Subquotient constructors}
1942
