2292	  \emph{(i) Abstract variable-legs restatement.} Restate the codomain read — and, with it, the
2293	  whole four-factor chain — for \emph{generic} morphisms \(g', f'\) carrying the cone-leg
2294	  equalities \(g' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\) and

2296	  projections \(\operatorname{pr}_1, \operatorname{pr}_2\). In this abstract form the legs are
2297	  free variables, not terms pinned by the pullback's universal property, so each can be
2298	  eliminated by substituting its defining equation: the equality proof \(w\), the adjunction
2299	  index, and the \(\gamma_{\psi}\)/\(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) arguments are all expressed in the
2300	  free leg variables and transform uniformly under the substitution. This restatement converts
2301	  the blocked rewrite into a legitimate one; it is the structural heart of the step.

2303	  \emph{(ii) \(\Gamma\)-collapse of the transparent coherences.} On global sections, read through
2304	  the \(\Gamma\)-pushforward dictionaries (Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso},
2305	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}), the two pushforward-composition factors and the
2306	  congruence factor are transparent. The composition coherences collapse to the identity —
2307	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{g',f})^{\mathrm{hom}}\) and
2308	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{f',g})^{\mathrm{inv}}\) evaluate, on the top open, to the identity
2309	  map on restriction of scalars (their section-level values are the identity) — and they
2310	  serve only to rebracket the iterated pushforward along the two factorizations
2311	  \((g' \circ f)_* = (f' \circ g)_*\) of the square's composite. (The
2312	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{g',f})^{\mathrm{hom}}\) collapse is a transparent re-association
2313	  that is most naturally accounted for together with the leg-reindex of step~(iii) rather than at
2314	  this point; the \(\Gamma\)-collapse identity itself is the same.) The congruence factor collapses
2315	  to a canonical transport along the equality of opens: its section-level value
2316	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\,w)^{\mathrm{hom}}\) is the
2317	  presheaf-restriction along the canonical equality of opens, i.e.\ a repackaging along
2318	  that equality carrying no substantive content. Thus after the \(\Gamma\)-collapse the only
2319	  surviving non-trivial factor is \(f_{*}\bigl(\eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}\bigr)\) read on global
2320	  sections.

2323	  (step~(i)) to \(g' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\), the leg-reindex engine

2330	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}) and the \(e\)-unit is invertible, hence an

2559	  In the situation of Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_inner_unitReduce}, post-composing the
2560	  four-factor distribution with the codomain identification \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) of
2561	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read} cancels three of the four factors against the
2562	  matching \(e\)-pieces baked into \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\), in the order:
2563	  \begin{enumerate}
2564	    \item the \(e\)-unit factor \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_{*}\eta^{e}\) is an isomorphism
2565	      (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_eUnit}) and cancels the inverse \(e\)-unit
2566	      built into \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\);
2567	    \item the surviving
2568	      \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})^{\mathrm{hom}}\) has identity
2569	      \(\Gamma\)-image (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pushforwardComp}) and drops;
2570	    \item the trailing
2571	      \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})^{\mathrm{hom}}\) cancels its inverse
2572	      inside \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\)
2573	      (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pullbackComp}).
2574	  \end{enumerate}
2575	  After cancellation, only the affine \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)\)-unit
2576	  \(\eta^{\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A}_{\widetilde M}\), conjugated by the affine tilde/\(\Gamma\)
2577	  dictionaries over \(\operatorname{Spec} A\), survives.
2578	\end{lemma}
2579	\begin{proof}
2580	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_eUnit,
2581	        lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pushforwardComp,
2582	        lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pullbackComp,
2583	        lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp, lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}
2584	  Distribute the bare \((g')\)-unit by the proved engine
2585	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp} (with \(a := e^{\mathrm{hom}}\),
2586	  \(b := \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\), \(N := \widetilde M\), so \(g' = e^{\mathrm{hom}} \circ
2587	  \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\)) and read through \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_{*}(-)\bigr)\)
2588	  by functoriality, giving the four ordered factors. Then apply the three atoms in turn:
2589	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_eUnit} cancels the \(e\)-unit against the inverse
2590	  \(e\)-unit of \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\); Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pushforwardComp}
2591	  drops the \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}\) factor; and
2592	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pullbackComp} cancels the \(\mathrm{pullbackComp}\)
2593	  factor against its inverse in \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\). The lone survivor is the affine
2594	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)\)-unit read through the affine dictionaries, which is the
2595	  load-bearing telescoping of the reindex.
2596	
2597	  \emph{Order of operations (load-bearing).} The unit distribution of
2598	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp} must be performed while \(g'\) is still presented as
2599	  the \emph{free composite} \(e^{\mathrm{hom}} \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\) — i.e.\ before the
2600	  generic-square legs \((g', f')\) are identified with the concrete pullback projections
2601	  \((\operatorname{pr}_1, \operatorname{pr}_2)\) by Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}.
2602	  Distributing first keeps each of the four factors in the free \(a\!\circ\!b\) shape against which the
2603	  three atoms are stated, so they apply directly. If instead one first reads through the codomain
2604	  identification (substituting the literal projection legs and collapsing the
2605	  \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}\) bookkeeping), the surviving \((g')\)-unit becomes the rigid literal
2606	  \((\mathrm{pullbackSpecIso})^{\mathrm{hom}} \circ \operatorname{Spec}(\mathrm{includeLeft})\), whose
2607	  shape no longer presents as an \((a\!\circ\!b)\)-unit and against which the atoms are inapplicable.
2608	  Hence: distribute the unit on the free composite first; cancel with the three atoms; \emph{then}
2609	  read through Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}.
2610	\end{proof}

2046	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Separatedness" (read from
2047	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860).
2048	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2049	  % "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
2050	  %  can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$
2051	  %  defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$,
2052	  %  and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
2053	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
2054	  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
2055	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is separated; equivalently, the diagonal
2056	  \(\Delta : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
2057	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a closed immersion.
2058	\end{lemma}
2059	
2060	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with
2061	% $U^I\times U^J$ can be seen to be the closed subscheme
2062	% $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
2063	% $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
2064	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860.)
2065	\begin{proof}
2066	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_surjective, lem:gr_separated_toSpecZ}
2067	  \leanok
2068	  The products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), as \(I, J\) range over the
2069	  size-\(d\) subsets, form an affine open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
2070	  \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Separatedness may be checked on this
2071	  cover: it suffices to show that for each pair \((I,J)\) the preimage
2072	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J)\) maps by a closed immersion into
2073	  \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), i.e.\ that the corresponding ring
2074	  homomorphism is surjective.
2075	
2076	  By \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} the diagonal preimage is the overlap
2077	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J) = U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J = \Spec
2078	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\). The restricted diagonal corresponds to the ring map
2079	  \[
2080	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
2081	    \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
2082	    \qquad
2083	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad 1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
2084	  \]
2085	  the second component being the comorphism \(\theta_{I,J}\) of
2086	  \cref{def:gr_transition} (a point of \(U^I_J\) and its image under
2087	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) are the two coordinates of a point of the diagonal). We claim
2088	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Its image contains all entries of \(X^I\)
2089	  (from the first factor). From the second factor it contains the entries of
2090	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\); taking the \(I\)-minor of this matrix, and using
2091	  \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\), the image contains the entries of \((X^I_J)^{-1}\),
2092	  and in particular
2093	  \[
2094	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J .
2095	  \]
2096	  Hence \(1/P^I_J\) lies in the image, so the image is all of
2097	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) and \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Concretely the
2098	  closed subscheme \(\Delta_{I,J} \subseteq U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) is cut
2099	  out by the entries of the matrix relation
2100	  \[
2101	    X^J_I \, X^I - X^J \;=\; 0,
2102	  \]
2103	  which on the diagonal holds because \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) forces
2104	  \(X^J_I = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) and therefore \(X^J_I X^I =
2105	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I = X^J\). As each restricted diagonal is a closed immersion,
2106	  \(\Delta\) is a closed immersion and \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over
2107	  \(\mathbb{Z}\).
2108	\end{proof}

2113	\begin{lemma}
2114	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is proper over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
2115	  \label{lem:gr_proper}
2116	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isProper}
2117	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated}
2118	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Properness" (read from
2119	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L865-L891).
2120	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2121	  % "We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper. It is enough to
2122	  %  verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.
2123	  %  Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
2124	  %  $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by
2125	  %  a ring homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$. Having fixed
2126	  %  one such $I$, next choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum,
2127	  %  where $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes
2128	  %  the discrete valuation. As $P^I_I = 1$, note that
2129	  %  $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore
2130	  %  $f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$ and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$
2131	  %  lies in $GL_d(\K)$.
2132	  %
2133	  %  Now consider the homomorphism
2134	  %  $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
2135	  %  $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$
2136	  %  Then $g$ defines the same morphism $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$,
2137	  %  and moreover all $d\times d$ minors $X^J_K$ satisfy
2138	  %  $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$.
2139	  %  As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it follows from the above that in fact
2140	  %  $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of $X^J$.
2141	  %  Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$
2142	  %  factors uniquely via $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of
2143	  %  schemes $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
2144	  %  $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked
2145	  %  separatedness of $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that
2146	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
2147	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
2148	  The structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
2149	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is proper.

2151	
2152	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is
2153	% proper. It is enough to verify the valuative criterion of properness for
2154	% discrete valuation rings. Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
2155	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by a ring
2156	% homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$. Having fixed one such $I$, next
2157	% choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum, where
2158	% $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes the discrete valuation. As
2159	% $P^I_I = 1$, note that $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore $f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$
2160	% and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$ lies in $GL_d(\K)$. Now consider the homomorphism
2161	% $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
2162	% $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$ Then $g$ defines the same morphism
2163	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$, and moreover all $d\times d$ minors
2164	% $X^J_K$ satisfy $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$. As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it
2165	% follows from the above that in fact $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of
2166	% $X^J$. Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ factors uniquely via
2167	% $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of schemes
2168	% $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
2169	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked separatedness of
2170	% $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
2171	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L865-L891.)
2172	\begin{proof}
2173	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated, def:gr_transition}
2174	  Since \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is of finite type
2175	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) and separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), and
2176	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) is Noetherian, properness follows from the valuative criterion
2177	  for discrete valuation rings: it suffices to show that every solid diagram
2178	  \[
2179	    \begin{array}{ccc}
2180	      \Spec K & \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } & \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \\
2181	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \pi \\
2182	      \Spec R & \longrightarrow & \Spec \mathbb{Z}
2183	    \end{array}
2184	  \]
2185	  with \(R\) a discrete valuation ring, \(K = \operatorname{Frac}(R)\) and
2186	  valuation \(\nu : K \to \mathbb{Z} \cup \{\infty\}\), admits a unique diagonal
2187	  filler \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
2188	
2189	  \emph{Existence.} The morphism \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) lands
2190	  in some chart \(U^I\) (\(\Spec K\) has one point), so it is given by a ring map
2191	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\). Among the \(\binom{r}{d}\) minors choose \(J\)
2192	  with \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) minimal. Because \(P^I_I = 1\) gives \(f(P^I_I) = 1\)
2193	  with \(\nu = 0\), the minimum satisfies \(\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0\); in particular
2194	  \(f(P^I_J) \ne 0\), so \(f(X^I_J) \in \mathrm{GL}_d(K)\) is invertible over
2195	  \(K\). Define
2196	  \[
2197	    g : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \longrightarrow K, \qquad
2198	    g(X^J) = f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr) ;
2199	  \]
2200	  this is the composite of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) with
2201	  \(f\), so \(g\) defines the same \(K\)-point \(\varphi\), now exhibited as
2202	  landing in \(U^J\).
2203	
2204	  We show every entry \(g(x^J_{p,q})\) lies in \(R\), i.e.\ has \(\nu \ge 0\).
2205	  First, for any size-\(d\) subset \(K' \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) the \(K'\)-minor
2206	  of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\), so
2207	  \[
2208	    g(P^J_{K'}) = \det f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\bigr)
2209	    = \frac{f(P^I_{K'})}{f(P^I_J)},
2210	    \qquad
2211	    \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) = \nu(f(P^I_{K'})) - \nu(f(P^I_J)) \ge 0
2212	  \]
2213	  by minimality of \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\). Now the \(J\)-minor \(X^J_J\) of \(X^J\) is
2214	  the identity, so by cofactor expansion each individual entry
2215	  \(x^J_{p,q}\) (with \(q \notin J\)) equals, up to sign, the minor
2216	  \(P^J_{K'}\) for \(K' = (J \setminus \{j_p\}) \cup \{q\}\) -- the \(d \times d\)
2217	  determinant obtained by replacing the \(p\)-th identity column of \(X^J_J\) by
2218	  the column \(q\). Hence \(\nu(g(x^J_{p,q})) = \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) \ge 0\) for all
2219	  entries, and the entries with \(q \in J\) are \(0\) or \(1\). Therefore
2220	  \(g(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]) \subseteq R\), so \(g\) factors uniquely as
2221	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R \hookrightarrow K\). The corresponding morphism
2222	  \(\Spec R \to U^J \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) restricts to \(\varphi\)
2223	  over \(\Spec K\) and lies over \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\), providing the filler.
2224	
2225	  \emph{Uniqueness.} Any two fillers agree over the generic point \(\Spec K\)
2226	  and, \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) being separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)
2227	  (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), two morphisms \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
2228	  agreeing on the schematically dense open \(\Spec K\) coincide. This verifies
2229	  the valuative criterion, so \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is
2230	  proper.
2231	\end{proof}

2233	\section{Out of scope}
2234	\label{sec:gr_out_of_scope}
2235	
2236	This chapter constructs the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
2237	the absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\) and establishes its smoothness, separatedness
2238	and properness. The following constructions are deferred to later chapters and
2239	are not treated here:
2240	\begin{itemize}
2241	  \item \textbf{The tautological/universal quotient} \(u : \mathcal{O}^{\oplus
2242	    r}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) and the determinant
2243	    line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\) with its Pl\"ucker embedding.
2244	  \item \textbf{The functor-of-points representability} of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
2245	    -- that it represents \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d) =
2246	    \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^r \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}} /
2247	    \mathbb{Z} / \mathbb{Z}}\); this is blueprinted as
2248	    \cref{thm:grassmannian_representable} in
2249	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}.
2250	  \item \textbf{The relative version over a general base \(S\)} with a locally
2251	    free sheaf \(V\) of rank \(r\): the scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V,d)\) obtained by
2252	    base change and gluing over a trivialising cover of \(V\). The
2253	    absolute-over-\(\mathbb{Z}\) construction of this chapter is the input to
2254	    that base change.
2255	\end{itemize}

1951	\begin{lemma}
1952	
1953	\leanok
1954	[The kernel pair's lower module is stable under the full family]
1955	  \label{lem:graded_ker_stable_full}
1956	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_stable_full}

2044	\subsubsection*{The degreewise difference identity}
2045	
2046	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2047	[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
2048	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
2049	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
2050	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2051	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2052	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
2053	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
2054	  \[
2055	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
2056	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
2057	  \]
2058	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\),
2059	  \(V = \mathcal{M}_n\), \(W = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi\) is the
2060	  degree-\(n\) piece \(K_n\) of the kernel subquotient and
2061	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x \mathcal{M}_n\) the
2062	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(C_{n+1}\) of the cokernel subquotient
2063	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, via the split \(G_1\)
2064	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq} and the degree shift
2065	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
2066	  \[
2067	    \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_n
2068	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
2069	  \]
2070	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
2071	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
2072	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
2073	\end{lemma}
2074	
2075	\begin{proof}
2076	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2077	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2078	    \leanok
2079	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
2080	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
2081	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
2082	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
2083	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
2084	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
2085	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
2086	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
2087	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
2088	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
2089	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
2090	  \(\ker(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\) and
2091	  \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}/\operatorname{range}(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\)
2092	  with the degree-\(n\) piece of the kernel subquotient and the degree-\((n+1)\)
2093	  piece of the cokernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
2094	  the latter because the degree-\((n+1)\) graded piece of the homogeneous submodule
2095	  \(x\mathcal{M}\) is exactly \(x \mathcal{M}_n\)
2096	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}).
2097	\end{proof}

2104	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2105	  Let \(Q\) be a \(\kappa\)-vector space that is also a module over

2111	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa) \cong \kappa\), a finite \(\kappa\)-module,
2112	  so finiteness of \(Q\) over it transfers to finiteness over \(\kappa\) along the scalar
2113	  tower. This is the length-zero input to the ambient subquotient induction
2114	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}): when \(r = 0\) the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a

2119	
2120	\leanok
2121	[independence of images modulo the kernel]
2122	  \label{lem:graded_iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup}
2123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup}
2124	  Let \(\pi : A \to Q\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map and \((g_i)_{i \in \iota}\) a family of
2125	  subspaces of \(A\) that is \emph{independent modulo \(\ker\pi\)}: for each \(i\), every
2126	  \(a \in g_i\) that also lies in \(\ker\pi + \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\) already lies in
2127	  \(\ker\pi\). Then the image family \((\pi(g_i))_{i \in \iota}\) is independent
2128	  (\(\mathrm{iSupIndep}\)) in \(Q\). Ring-agnostic lattice core of the base-case
2129	  independence step below; applied to the quotient map \(N \twoheadrightarrow N/N'\).
2130	\end{lemma}
2131	\begin{proof}
2132	
2133	\leanok
2134	  By the disjointness criterion for independence it suffices to show, for each \(i\),
2135	  that \(\pi(g_i) \cap \sum_{j \ne i} \pi(g_j) = 0\). Take \(q\) in that intersection;
2136	  write \(q = \pi(a)\) with \(a \in g_i\), and (pushing the sum through \(\pi\), since
2137	  \(\pi(\sum_{j\ne i} g_j) = \sum_{j\ne i}\pi(g_j)\)) \(q = \pi(b)\) with
2138	  \(b \in \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\). Then \(a - b \in \ker\pi\), so
2139	  \(a = (a-b) + b \in \ker\pi + \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\). By the hypothesis applied at \(i\),
2140	  \(a \in \ker\pi\), whence \(q = \pi(a) = 0\).
2141	\end{proof}

2758	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs}) at the projection legs

2760	  read Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read} is definitionally the leg-parametrised read,

2763	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor}; the reindex therefore evaluates

2766	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)\)-unit value of Seam~1

2773	  the literal projection legs the bare \((g')\)-unit cannot be expanded in place: the leg
2774	  \(\operatorname{pr}_1\) is only propositionally — not definitionally — equal to the composite

2786	[(B) Generator close: the base change of $\rho$ is the inverse regrouping]
2787	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_gstar_generator_close}
2788	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_gstar_generator_close}
2789	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv, def:base_change_mate_inner_value}
2790	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma `lemma-affine-base-change'
2791	  % (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the "boils down to the equality" step
2792	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L933--938).
2793	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the equality
2794	  % $$ (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M $$ as $R'$-modules."
2795	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base change''
2796	  (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
2797	  Let \(\rho : M \to (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M\), \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\), be the
2798	  inner comparison value of Definition~\ref{def:base_change_mate_inner_value}. The extension of
2799	  scalars along \(\psi : R \to R'\) of \(\rho\), post-composed with the algebraic counit
2800	  \(\varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}\) of the extension/restriction-of-scalars adjunction along \(\psi\),
2801	  is an \(R'\)-linear map \(R' \otimes_R M \to (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M\); on the generator
2802	  \(r' \otimes m\) it returns \(r' \cdot ((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m) = (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\),
2803	  which is exactly the value of the inverse regrouping isomorphism
2804	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}) on \(r' \otimes m\). Both maps being
2805	  \(R'\)-linear and agreeing on the generators \(r' \otimes m\), they coincide:
2806	  \[
2807	    \operatorname{ext}_\psi(\rho) \,\circ\, \varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}
2808	      \;=\; \operatorname{regroup}^{-1}.
2809	  \]
2810	\end{lemma}
2811	\begin{proof}
2812	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv, def:base_change_mate_inner_value}
2813	  Both sides are \(R'\)-linear maps out of \(R' \otimes_R M\), so by extensionality it suffices to
2814	  check the value on the generators \(r' \otimes m\). The left side sends
2815	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto r' \cdot \rho(m) = r' \cdot ((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m) =
2816	  (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\), using that \(\varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}\) reads the extension of
2817	  scalars by acting through the \(R'\)-scalar. The inverse regrouping isomorphism of
2818	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv} sends, by definition,
2819	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\) (in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation). The two
2820	  values agree on every generator, so the maps are equal. No flatness hypothesis is used.
2821	\end{proof}

3055	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv},

3057	  = \operatorname{regroup}^{-1}\), and in particular an isomorphism with \emph{no}
3058	  flatness hypothesis.

3065	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose}, with the domain and codomain pinned by
3066	  the domain read Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read} (\(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\)) and
3067	  the codomain read Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read} (\(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\)).

3070	  \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) (with \(g = \operatorname{Spec}\psi\)); by the counit form of the

3072	  \(\varepsilon_g\), with no opaque transpose remaining,
3073	  \(\Gamma(\theta) = \Gamma\bigl(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\bigr) \circ \Gamma(\varepsilon_g)\).

3075	  \[
3076	    \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1} \circ \Gamma(\theta) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}
3077	      = \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1} \circ

3079	        \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}

3082	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose}, which states it equals

3087	  equality'' \((A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M\) of the source, with no
3088	  flatness hypothesis used anywhere.

2207	  %   No outgoing linear map out of Q₀ is constructed, so no scalar ring other than κ
2208	  %   is ever introduced.
2209	  % NOTE: ROUTE (a). Building a κ-linear detector
2210	  %   Φ : Q₀ → M/N', [m] ↦ [π_n m] (degree-n projection) via descent through the
2211	  %   subquotient fails: the descended map carries the scalar action of Q₀'s base
2212	  %   ring, but the target M/N' carries only a κ-module structure -- a scalar-ring
2213	  %   incompatibility. Route (b) avoids this by working entirely within Q₀.
2214	
2215	  \emph{Step 2: finiteness of the support.} Because \(Q_0\) is finite-dimensional and
2216	  the ranges \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\) are independent, only finitely many of
2217	  them are nonzero: the set \(\{\,n : \operatorname{range}(\psi_n) \ne 0\,\}\) is
2218	  finite (\cref{lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib}). In particular it is bounded
2219	  above, by some \(K \in \mathbb{N}\).
2220	
2221	  \emph{Step 3: eventual vanishing.} For \(n > K\) we have
2222	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n) = 0\), i.e.\ every homogeneous element of
2223	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\) has class \(0\) in \(Q_0 = N/N'\); equivalently
2224	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N'\), hence \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n = N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)
2225	  and
2226	  \[
2227	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
2228	      - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n) \;=\; 0 .
2229	  \]
2230	  Thus \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero, and
2231	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
2232	\end{proof}

2236	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2237	[Rationality of the ambient subquotient Hilbert function]
2238	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
2239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_hilbertSeries_rational}
2240	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2241	    def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker, def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker,
2242	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
2243	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
2244	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
2245	    lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero,
2246	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2247	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
2248	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
2249	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
2250	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (induction on the number of
2251	  degree-one generators).}
2252	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r\)
2253	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with ambient Hilbert function
2254	  \(\mathrm{hilb}\). Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\)
2255	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}): the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
2256	  - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), the coefficient
2257	  sequence of a rational series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-r}\) with \(p \in
2258	  \mathbb{Q}[X]\). The induction is on the length \(r\), and every object that
2259	  appears is an ambient pair of homogeneous submodules of the fixed graded module
2260	  \(M\).
2261	\end{lemma}
2262	
2263	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, induction set-up (read
2264	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
2265	% "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
2266	%  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
2267	%  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds.
2268	%  To prove the induction step, let $x\in S_1$
2269	%  be one of a minimal set of generators, such that
2270	%  the induction hypothesis applies to the
2271	%  graded ring $S/(x)$."
2272	\begin{proof}
2273	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2274	    def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker, def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker,
2275	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
2276	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
2277	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
2278	    lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero,
2279	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2280	    \leanok
2281	  We induct on the length \(r\).
2282	
2283	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} A length-zero datum has a finiteness witness making the
2284	  subquotient \(N/N'\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space
2285	  (\cref{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}); its degreewise
2286	  images form an independent family inside that finite-dimensional space, so only
2287	  finitely many are nonzero and \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for \(n \gg 0\)
2288	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}). By
2289	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} an eventually-zero function is rational of order
2290	  \(0\), giving \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
2291	
2292	  \emph{Inductive step \(r \ge 1\).} Set \(x = t_{r-1}\). By the kernel/cokernel
2293	  closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) the kernel subquotient
2294	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel subquotient
2295	  \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) are subquotient data of length \(r-1\), assembled by
2296	  the kernel and cokernel constructors (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker},
2297	  \cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}) whose finiteness witnesses come from the
2298	  abstract finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}). The
2299	  induction hypothesis applied
2300	  to \(K\) and \(C\) gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and
2301	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}). By the degreewise
2302	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
2303	  \[
2304	    \mathrm{hilb}(n+1) - \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)
2305	    \qquad \text{for all } n .
2306	  \]
2307	  Feeding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\), \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and this
2308	  difference identity into the inductive-step engine
2309	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}) yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\). (The
2310	  common order \(r-1\) for \(h_C\) and \(h_K\) is arranged, if necessary, by raising
2311	  the lower one with \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump}.) This completes the induction.
2312	\end{proof}

2314	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
2315	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
2316	
2317	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
2318	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
2319	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
2320	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
2321	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
2322	
2323	\begin{definition}
2324	
2325	\leanok
2326	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
2327	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
2328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
2329	  % NOTE: The definition carries no \uses: the Lean construction is
2330	  % `IdealSheafData.ofIdeals (fun U => Module.annihilator Γ(X,U) Γ(F,U))`, which is well-defined
2331	  % unconditionally and does NOT depend on the localization bridges at definition time. The two
2332	  % facts lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map and lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen are needed
2333	  % only for the downstream CHARACTERIZATION (the reverse inclusion / equality on affine opens, the
2334	  % "annihilator forward direction"), which is not yet a blueprint block.
2335	  % NOTE: The definition itself is formalized and axiom-clean; the inclusion direction
2336	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
2337	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
2338	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
2339	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
2340	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
2341	  % Polynomials" (read from
2342	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
2343	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2344	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
2345	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
2346	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
2347	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
2348	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2349	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
2350	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
2351	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
2352	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
2353	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
2354	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
2355	  \(U \subseteq X\),
2356	  \[
2357	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2358	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
2359	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
2360	  \]
2361	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
2362	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
2363	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
2364	
2365	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
2366	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
2367	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
2368	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
2369	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
2370	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
2371	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
2372	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
2373	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
2374	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
2375	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
2376	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2377	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
2378	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
2379	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
2380	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2381	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
2382	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
2383	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
2384	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
2385	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
2386	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
2387	
2388	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
2389	\end{definition}
2390	
2391	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2392	[Annihilator ideal bounded by the module annihilator on affine opens]
2393	  \label{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}
2394	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal_le}
2395	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}
2396	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and
2397	  \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then the section over \(U\) of the annihilator
2398	  ideal sheaf \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}) is
2399	  contained in the module-annihilator of the sections of \(\mathcal{F}\) over
2400	  \(U\):
2401	  \[
2402	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2403	    \;\subseteq\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr).
2404	  \]
2405	  This is the inclusion direction available directly from the definition of
2406	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\); the reverse inclusion is the basic-open coherence
2407	  statement gated on \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}. Proved directly
2408	  in Lean. It supports the well-definedness of the schematic support
2409	  (\cref{def:schematic_support}).
2410	\end{lemma}
2411	
2412	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2413	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
2414	  localization]
2415	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
2416	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
2417	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
2418	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
2419	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
2420	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
2421	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
2422	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
2423	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
2424	  \[
2425	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
2426	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
2427	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
2428	  \]
2429	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
2430	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
2431	
2432	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
2433	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
2434	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
2435	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
2436	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
2437	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
2438	\end{lemma}
2439	
2440	\begin{proof}
2441	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
2442	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
2443	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
2444	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
2445	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
2446	
2447	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
2448	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
2449	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
2450	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
2451	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
2452	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
2453	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
2454	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
2455	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
2456	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
2457	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
2458	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
2459	\end{proof}

2461	\begin{lemma}[Sections on a basic open localize the affine ring]
2462	  \label{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2463	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen}

2466	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).}

2468	  \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then the restriction map
2469	  \(\Gamma(X, U) \to \Gamma\bigl(X,\, D(f)\bigr)\) on the basic open

2477	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2478	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen}

2497	  % SOURCE QUOTE:

2505	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
2506	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
2507	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
2508	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:

2522	\begin{proof}
2523	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2524	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
2525	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
2526	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).

2528	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
2529	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
2530	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
2531	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
2532	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
2533	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
2534	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
2535	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
2536	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
2537	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
2538	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
2539	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
2540	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
2541	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
2542	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
2543	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
2544	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
2545	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.

3673	    Being an isomorphism is local on \(S'\), so we may take \(S = \operatorname{Spec} A\)
3674	    and \(S' = \operatorname{Spec} B\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
3675	    quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on \(S\)
3676	    (Stacks Tag 01XJ / Mathlib's quasi-coherence of the pushforward under a
3677	    quasi-compact quasi-separated morphism), hence is the sheaf associated to the
3678	    \(A\)-module \(\Gamma(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = \Gamma(X,\mathcal{F})\); likewise
3679	    \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is associated to \(\Gamma(X_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\). Pullback along
3680	    \(g\) corresponds to \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, so under the
3681	    tilde-equivalence the sheaf base-change map is the tilde of the comparison map
3682	    \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \to \Gamma(X_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\); since
3683	    \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is fully faithful on quasi-coherent modules, one is an
3684	    isomorphism iff the other is.
3685	  \end{proof}
3686	\end{lemma}

3688	\begin{theorem}\leanok
3689	[Flat base change, $i=0$ case]
3690	  \label{thm:flat_base_change_pushforward}

3692	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward,
3693	        lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib,
3694	        lem:flat_base_change_reduce_global_sections,
3695	        lem:flat_base_change_mayer_vietoris}
3696	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH (Lemma "Flat base change", part for
3697	  % $i = 0$) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947--970, and
3698	  % references/stacks-coherent.md).
3699	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
3700	  % $$
3701	  % \xymatrix{
3702	  % X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
3703	  % S' \ar[r]^g & S
3704	  % }
3705	  % $$
3706	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
3707	  % with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
3708	  % Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
3709	  % For any $i \geq 0$
3710	  % \begin{enumerate}
3711	  % \item the base change map of
3712	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
3713	  % is an isomorphism
3714	  % $$
3715	  % g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
3716	  % $$
3717	  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
3718	  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
3719	  % \end{enumerate}"
3720	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the
3721	  \(i = 0\) case.}
3722	  Consider the cartesian square
3723	  \[
3724	    \begin{array}{ccc}
3725	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
3726	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
3727	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
3728	    \end{array}
3729	  \]
3730	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
3731	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\)
3732	  is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of
3733	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
3734	  \[
3735	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
3736	    f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
3737	  \]
3738	  Equivalently, in the affine situation \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
3739	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map
3740	  \[
3741	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
3742	    H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
3743	  \]
3744	  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
3745	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
3746	  \(\mathcal{F}\).
3747	
3748	  The hypothesis that \(\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent is essential: it is what
3749	  makes \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent on the affine base and thus the tilde of
3750	  a module, and it is what lets the affine reduction
3751	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) write \(\mathcal{F}\) as
3752	  \(\widetilde{M}\) over each affine open. Without it the comparison map need not be
3753	  an isomorphism.
3754	\end{theorem}

3826	  For \(i = 0\) the argument is {\v C}ech-\emph{free}: it uses only the sheaf
3827	  condition (the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) part of {\v C}ech, i.e.\ an equalizer of a finite
3828	  cover), the affine lemma, and the fact that flat base change commutes with that
3829	  finite equalizer. There is no Leray sequence and no {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
3830	  sequence. The proof is the composite of the decomposition chain.

3832	  \medskip
3833	  \emph{Reduction to the global-sections comparison.} By
3834	  Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_reduce_global_sections}, the base-change map of
3835	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism if and only if,
3836	  after reducing to affine bases \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),

3838	  \[

3840	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
3841	  \]
3842	  is an isomorphism (this uses that \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent — \(f\) is
3843	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated — so each side is the tilde of its module of
3844	  global sections, and that pullback along \(g\) is \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules).
3845	
3846	  \medskip
3847	  \emph{The comparison map is an isomorphism.} Since \(f\) is quasi-compact and
3848	  quasi-separated, \(X\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated over \(A\), so
3849	  Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_mayer_vietoris} applies and gives
3850	  \[
3851	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
3852	  \]
3853	  Internally that lemma presents \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F})\) as the finite
3854	  sheaf-condition equalizer of a finite affine cover
3855	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer}), base changes the equalizer diagram term by
3856	  term via the affine lemma Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
3857	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_changed_equalizer_diagram}), and commutes \(- \otimes_A B\)
3858	  past that finite equalizer by flatness
3859	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}), reducing the general
3860	  quasi-separated case to the separated case
3861	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_separated}) by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the
3862	  number of cover members. Combined with the reduction above, the sheaf-level
3863	  base-change map is an isomorphism.
3864	\end{proof}

3866	\subsection{The \texorpdfstring{$A$}{A}-module \texorpdfstring{$\operatorname{eqLocus}$}{eqLocus} globalization (FBC-B)}
3867	
3868	The separated-case argument is repackaged here at the level of \(A\)-modules, where
3869	\(A = \Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is the ring of global sections of the structure sheaf, so that flat
3870	base change becomes the Mathlib equivalence \(\operatorname{tensorEqLocusEquiv}\)
3871	(Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}) applied to an honest
3872	\(\operatorname{LinearMap.eqLocus}\). The blocks below construct that presentation concretely: every
3873	module of sections \(\Gamma(U, M)\) is viewed as an \(A\)-module by restriction of scalars along the
3874	structure-sheaf restriction \(A \to \Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X)\), the restriction maps of \(M\) become
3875	\(A\)-linear, and the two product legs of the sheaf condition assemble into a pair of \(A\)-linear maps
3876	whose equalizer locus is \(\Gamma(X, M)\). Throughout, \(M : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) is a sheaf of
3877	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) is an open cover with
3878	\(\bigvee_i U_i = \top\).
3879	
3880	The bijectivity of the keystone presentation rests on two element-level sheaf axioms of the underlying
3881	abelian-group sheaf \(M.\mathrm{presheaf}\), recorded as Mathlib dependency anchors.
3882	
3883	\begin{lemma}[Sheaf separatedness: local equality forces global equality]
3884	  \label{lem:sheaf_eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}
3885	  \lean{TopCat.Sheaf.eq_of_locally_eq'}
3886	  \mathlibok
3887	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
3888	  Let \(\mathcal{G}\) be a sheaf on a topological space \(X\), \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) a
3889	  family covering an open \(V\) (i.e.\ \(V \le \bigvee_i U_i\)), and \(s, t \in \mathcal{G}(V)\) two
3890	  sections. If the restrictions of \(s\) and \(t\) to every \(U_i\) agree, then \(s = t\). This is the
3891	  separatedness half of the sheaf condition, stated element-wise.
3892	\end{lemma}
3893	
3894	\begin{lemma}[Sheaf gluing: a compatible family has a unique amalgamation]
3895	  \label{lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
3896	  \lean{TopCat.Sheaf.existsUnique_gluing'}
3897	  \mathlibok
3898	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
3899	  Let \(\mathcal{G}\) be a sheaf on \(X\), \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) a family covering an open
3900	  \(V\), and \((s_i)_i\) a family of sections \(s_i \in \mathcal{G}(U_i)\) that is \emph{compatible}
3901	  (the restrictions of \(s_i\) and \(s_j\) to \(U_i \cap U_j\) agree for all \(i, j\)). Then there is a
3902	  unique \(s \in \mathcal{G}(V)\) whose restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(s_i\). This is the gluing half
3903	  of the sheaf condition, stated element-wise.
3904	\end{lemma}

3906	\begin{definition}[The ground ring of global sections]
3907	  \label{def:fbcb_groundRing}
3908	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.groundRing}
3909	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{ground ring} is the commutative ring
3910	  \(A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = \mathcal{O}_X(\top)\) of global sections of the structure sheaf,
3911	  taken from the \(\mathbf{CommRing}\)-valued structure presheaf so its ring and algebra structures
3912	  resolve canonically.
3913	\end{definition}
3914	
3915	\begin{definition}[The structure-sheaf restriction ring map \texorpdfstring{$A \to \Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X)$}{A to sections over U}]
3916	  \label{def:fbcb_rhoU}
3917	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rhoU}
3918	  \uses{def:fbcb_groundRing}
3919	  For an open \(U \subseteq X\), the structure-sheaf restriction along \(U \subseteq \top\) is a ring
3920	  homomorphism \(\rho_U : A = \Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X) \to \Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the ring map
3921	  along which sections over \(U\) are regarded as \(A\)-modules.
3922	\end{definition}
3923	
3924	\begin{lemma}[Transitivity of the restriction ring maps]
3925	  \label{lem:fbcb_rhoU_comp}
3926	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rhoU_comp}
3927	  \uses{def:fbcb_rhoU}
3928	  For opens \(V \le U \subseteq X\), the structure-sheaf restriction
3929	  \(\Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X) \to \Gamma(V,\mathcal{O}_X)\) composed with \(\rho_U\) equals \(\rho_V\):
3930	  \[
3931	    \bigl(\Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X) \to \Gamma(V,\mathcal{O}_X)\bigr) \circ \rho_U = \rho_V .
3932	  \]
3933	  \begin{proof}
3934	    \uses{def:fbcb_rhoU}
3935	    Both sides are the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X) \to \Gamma(V,\mathcal{O}_X)\)
3936	    along \(V \subseteq \top\), factored as \(V \subseteq U \subseteq \top\); functoriality of the
3937	    structure presheaf collapses the two-step restriction to the one-step restriction.
3938	  \end{proof}
3939	\end{lemma}

3941	\begin{definition}[Sections over \texorpdfstring{$U$}{U} as an \texorpdfstring{$A$}{A}-module]

3969	\begin{lemma}[The \texorpdfstring{$A$}{A}-linear restriction is the underlying presheaf restriction]
